*Includes people in prison for new offenses and with sentence lengths that are not less than one year or life sentences.
::: {.panel-tabset .nav-pills} # Parole Eligibility ## How Parole Eligibility is Determined Parole eligibility refers to the point in a person's prison sentence when they become eligible for consideration for release under parole. Parole is a conditional release, allowing the person to serve the remainder of their sentence in the community under supervision, rather than in prison. The parole board by policy sets parole eligibility for many offenses: A maximum sentence of 40 years or more is eligible in 8 years; maximum terms of two to 39 years are eligible at the 20-percent; and sentences of one year and up to but not including two years are eligible at 4 months. By statute there is a 15 percent group for nonviolent Class D offenses and an 85 percent group for violent offenses.
## In Prison Past Their Parole Eligibility In 2020, 61 percent of people in prison were in prison past their parole eligibility. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: From 2010 to 2020, the percent of people* in prison past parole eligibility increased by 22 percent. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: :::
### Demographics ::: {.panel-tabset .custom-tabs} #### Race and Ethnicity In 2020, 73 percent of people* in prison past parole eligibility were White, non-Hispanic people. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: #### Gender In 2020, 90 percent of people* in prison past parole eligibility were males. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: #### Age In 2020, 36 percent of people* in prison past parole eligibility were between the ages of 25 to 34 years old. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: :::
### Offense Types In 2020, 43 percent of people* in prison past parole eligibility were in prison for violent offenses and 53 percent for non-violent offenses. Most people who were incarcerated past parole eligibility were serving time for drugs (21%) and property (19%) offenses. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: :::
### Sentence Lengths In 2020, 52 percent of people* in prison past parole eligibility had sentence lengths between 10 to 24.9 years. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: :::
## State Notes "What if we change the section from Missing Data to more of a high-level state-specific Methods section? It can include: 1) Total N for the state 2) How we determined parole eligibility (e.g., what formulas did we use to estimate parole eligibility year) 3) Missing data patterns" - Jess S. # Prison Population ## Prison Population Trends From 2010 to 2022, the prison population decreased 10 percent, changing from 20,544 in 2010 to 18,560 in 2022. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: :::
## Demographics ::: {.panel-tabset .custom-tabs} #### Race and Ethnicity In 2022, NA percent of people in prison were Black, non-Hispanic people. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: #### Gender In 2022, 88 percent of people in prison were males. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: #### Age In 2022, 33 percent of people in prison were between the ages of 35 to 44 years old. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: :::
## Offense Types In 2020, 24 percent of people in prison were incarcerated for drugs offenses. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: :::
## Sentence Lengths In 2020, 40 percent of people in prison had sentence lengths between 10 to 24.9 years. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: # Prison Releases ## Prison Release Trends From 2010 to 2020, prison releases increased 2 percent, dropping from 16,520 in 2010 to 16,868 in 2020. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: :::
In 2020, 57 percent of people eligible for parole were released during their eligibility year. This represents a 1 percent increase compared to 2010. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: :::
## Conditional Releases Conditional release involves an individual’s release under specific conditions and supervision, whereas unconditional release means the individual is released without further obligations or restrictions. In 2020, 72 percent of prison releases were conditional releases. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: :::
## Demographics ::: {.panel-tabset .custom-tabs} #### Race and Ethnicity In 2020, 83 percent of prison releases were White, non-Hispanic people. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: #### Gender In 2020, 80 percent of prison releases were males. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: #### Age In 2020, 36 percent of prison releases were between the ages of 25 to 34 years old. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: :::
## Offense Types In 2020, 40 percent of prison releases were for people incarcerated for drugs offenses. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: # Disparities ## Disparities in Incarceration ::: {.panel-tabset .custom-tabs} ### Race and Ethnicity Racial and ethnic disparities in prison incarceration represents the accumulation of disparities across the system.
::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](../../Users/mroberts/The Council of State Governments/JC Research - Documents/RES_Parole/data/analysis/app/rri_infographic_black_Kentucky.png){width=1188} ::: ::: In 2020, Black people were incarcerated in state prison at a rate 3.2 times higher than White people, when accounting for population sizes in Kentucky. ::: {.cell} ::: ### Gender Gender disparities in prison incarceration represents the accumulation of disparities across the system.
:::
## Time Served ::: {.panel-tabset .custom-tabs} ### Race and Ethnicity In 2020, Black people spent 0.8 more years on average in prison, and Hispanic people spent 0.2 more years on average in prison compared to White people. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: This chart shows the average time served in prison for the most serious sentenced offense by race and ethnicity in 2020. The largest disparity was observed among rape or sexual assault offenses, where Black people spent 2.7 more years in prison on average compared to people of Other race(s), who had the shortest time served for these offenses. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: ### Gender In 2020, females spent 0.6 years fewer on average in prison compared to males in Kentucky. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: This chart shows the average time served in prison for the most serious sentenced offense by gender in 2020. For rape or sexual assault offenses, males spent 2.7 more years in prison on average compared to females. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: :::
## Disparities in Parole Eligibility ::: {.panel-tabset .custom-tabs} ### Race and Ethnicity
::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](../../Users/mroberts/The Council of State Governments/JC Research - Documents/RES_Parole/data/analysis/app/pe_rri_infographic_black_Kentucky.png){width=1188} ::: ::: In 2020, Black people were incarcerated in state prison past parole eligibility at a rate 3.5 times higher than White people, when accounting for population sizes in Kentucky. ::: {.cell} ::: ### Gender :::

The largest disparity observed was for rape or sexual assault offenses, where people of Black, non-Hispanic had 3 more years in prison past parole eligibility on average compared to Hispanic people people, who had the shortest time in prison past parole eligibility for these offenses. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

For rape or sexual assault offenses, males spent 2 more years in prison past parole eligibility on average compared to females. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: :::
## How Disparities Are Calculated While White, Black, and Hispanic are not the only race and ethnicity categories found in the U.S., NCRP only provides four categories: White, Black, Hispanic, and Other. Further categories will be added in future data collections by the CSG Justice Center. Additionally, the term Hispanic is used here to maintain consistency with the data source material. State data broken down by race and ethnicity were pulled from National Corrections Reporting Program, 1991-2020: Selected Variables (NCRP). Then, the rate of people readmitted to parole was calculated within each racial and ethnic group based on the Census Public Use Microdata Sample (PUMS). The relative rate index is calculated by dividing the rate for Black individuals or Hispanic individuals by the rate for White individuals. *States vary in how they collect and report information about race and ethnicity. Some states use the race or ethnicity recorded by intake officers, while other states allow individuals to identify their own race and ethnicity. Furthermore, some states combine race and ethnicity into one category, while others separate them into two distinct items. These differences can result in an incomplete count of Hispanic individuals and those of mixed race in state reports. The US Census allows individuals to self-identify their race and ethnicity separately. The variation in practices across states and between state and Federal data reporting systems may result in a deflated RRI for Hispanic individuals. These differences can result in an incomplete count of Hispanic individuals and those of mixed race in state reports. Conversely, it can result in an overcount of people of other races, especially non-Hispanic White people.* :::
## End Notes Data analysis was conducted using information from the National Corrections Reporting Program and the National Prisoner Statistics Program. See “National Corrections Reporting Program (NCRP),” Bureau of Justice Statistics, accessed September 25, 2024, https://bjs.ojp.gov/data-collection/national-corrections-reporting-program-ncrp; “National Prisoner Statistics (NPS) Program,” Bureau of Justice Statistics, accessed September 25, 2024, https://bjs.ojp.gov/data-collection/national-prisoner-statistics-nps-program.
